data.title: China cites Biden’s message to Xi that Japan could build nuclear weapons ‘overnight’
data.description:
China released an official report on Thursday stating that Japan “may already have produced weapons-grade plutonium in secret” and “has the technological and economic capabilities to achieve nuclear armament in a short period of time”.
The report assessing Japan’s nuclear threat cited former US president Joe Biden’s direct message to Chinese President Xi Jinping that Japan had the capacity to have nuclear weapons “virtually overnight”.
Biden first disclosed the information in an interview with the American public broadcaster Public Broadcasting Service in June 2016.
“What happens if Japan, who could tomorrow, could go nuclear tomorrow? They have the capacity to do it virtually overnight,” Biden said when recalling a conversation he had with Xi, but he did not say when it took place.


Although some state media reported the interview, Chinese authorities have not responded to Biden’s remarks in public.
On Thursday, the 30-page report titled “Nuclear Ambitions of Japan’s Right-Wing Forces: A Serious Threat to World Peace” was released in Beijing and included a call for the international community to “take concrete and strong measures” against Japan’s escalating nuclear ambitions.
It comes as relations between China and Japan are at their lowest point in years. A senior Japanese official last month told local media that he believed Japan should own nuclear weapons, but Tokyo denied the claim represented Japan’s official stance.
The report was produced jointly by the China Arms Control and Disarmament Association (CACDA) and the Nuclear Strategic Planning Research Institute, a think tank affiliated with the China National Nuclear Corporation.
It suggested that Japan had secretly pursued the research and development of nuclear weapons during the second world war. “[Japan] has since established a comprehensive nuclear fuel cycle system and possesses robust nuclear industrial capabilities,” the report said.
The authors specifically highlighted Japan’s capabilities in manufacturing plutonium, a critical material for making bombs.
The report said Japan was the only non-nuclear state under the NPT (Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty) with spent nuclear fuel reprocessing technology and the capability to extract weapons-grade plutonium, which is recovered from uranium fuel irradiated for only two to three months in a plutonium production reactor.
“According to publicly available information from the Japan Atomic Energy Agency, Japan reprocessed spent fuel from the experimental fast reactor Joyo in 1984 to recover plutonium. The reactor’s initial core design included a breeding zone, and from its operation beginning in 1978 until the core design was changed in 1994, it technically had the potential to produce weapons-grade plutonium,” the report said.
“US experts have analysed that Japan likely used the Joyo experimental fast reactor to produce approximately 40kg [88lbs] of weapons-grade plutonium before the core design was modified,” it said, citing a 1994 Greenpeace study.
The report also said Japan had stockpiled plutonium far exceeding its needs for civilian nuclear power, creating what it said was a persistent and severe imbalance between the supply and demand of sensitive nuclear materials.
Furthermore, it assessed that Japan maintained operational platforms with nuclear weapons delivery capabilities and had the technological foundation to develop nuclear-powered submarines and aircraft carriers.
For decades, Japan has adhered to three non-nuclear principles – not possessing, producing or permitting the introduction of nuclear weapons – while relying on the United States for extended deterrence. Japan is also a signatory to the NPT, which commits non-nuclear states to forgo the development or acquisition of nuclear arms.
At the launch event for the report, Dai Huaicheng, CACDA’s secretary general, said that “for some time now, Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi has hinted at revising the three non-nuclear principles, with senior Japanese officials even going so far as to claim that Japan should possess nuclear weapons”.
Dai said such remarks revealed Japan’s ambitions “to break free from the constraints of the international order and accelerate the revival of militarism and remilitarisation”.
At a daily press briefing on Thursday, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said the report exposed the intention of Japanese right-wing forces to possess nuclear weapons. She said the report listed “the alarming facts” that Japan had secretly been conducting research on nuclear weapons and stockpiling large amounts of sensitive nuclear materials for a long time.
Some Chinese nuclear experts estimate that Japan has the political motivation, as well as the technical capacity, to develop nuclear weapons in less than three years.


An expert based in western China who asked not to be named due to the sensitivity of the topic said there was speculation that Japan already had two nuclear bombs.
“If they dare to pursue nuclear weapons, that number could grow to three or four. And nowadays, the yield of just one tactical nuclear weapon is 10 times that of the two bombs they once received,” he said, referring to the bombing of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in World War II.
On Tuesday, China’s Ministry of Commerce announced a ban on the export of “dual-use” products to end users linked to the Japanese military. The export of items with both commercial and military applications which could “contribute to enhancing Japan’s military capabilities” is prohibited.
A Beijing-based nuclear expert, who was not authorised to speak to the media and also requested anonymity, said on Friday: “The government has clearly recognised the urgency of the situation. If we don’t act now, Japan will go nuclear”.

data.title: Why From Dusk Till Dawn has aged poorly as the vampire movie with George Clooney turns 30
data.description:
This is the latest instalment in our From the Vault feature series, in which we reflect on culturally significant movies celebrating notable anniversaries.
In recent months, Quentin Tarantino has been in the news for criticising actors such as Paul Dano and Matthew Lillard in a manner unbecoming of a cinephile. But there was a time he fancied himself a leading man – or at least, the leading man’s creepy brother.
Directed by Robert Rodriguez (Desperado), from a script by Tarantino – based on a treatment by special effects expert Robert Kurtzman – From Dusk Till Dawn turns 30 this month. Although it contains all the hallmarks of a typical 1990s Tarantino crime caper, it switches gears halfway, morphing into a gory vampire flick.
George Clooney, still on his way to mega-stardom, plays bank robber Seth Gecko, on the run after a jailbreak staged by his brother, Richie (Tarantino). Even with a tattoo snaking up his neck, Clooney does not quite convince as a cold-blooded killer, but his charisma goes some way to masking the movie’s flaws.


Surprisingly, for those who have suffered through his cameos, Tarantino is well cast as the psychotic sex offender Richie. But compared to Clooney’s effortless star wattage, he is still, to quote his assessment of Dano, “weak sauce”.
If Tarantino casting himself as a sex offender seems strange, Rodriguez compounds the problem by having the camera mimic Richie’s malevolent gaze. The fact that their boss was Miramax’s Harvey Weinstein makes the decision even more uncomfortable.
But then, From Dusk Till Dawn always plays to the lowest common denominator, moving from juvenile to downright sleazy. One wonders what the ever-classy Clooney made of it all.
The film starts in familiar Tarantino territory with the Geckos and their hostage Gloria (Brenda Hillhouse) hiding from the cops at a Texas roadside store.

Anchored by a long back-and-forth between a Texas Ranger (Michael Parks) and the store owner (John Hawkes) that turns into a larger-than-life shoot-out, this opening sequence is tense and funny, with witty reversals, profane dialogue and plenty of pop culture references.
While nothing hints at the supernatural carnage to come, watching the owner firing a gun while engulfed in flames suggests there will be no holding back.
Sure enough, the next sequence has Richie, left alone with the hostage at a motel, sexually assaulting and killing her off-screen. “That’s not how it’s done!” Seth shouts, as if Richie has broken the criminals’ code rather than committed an unforgivable act of violence.
Perhaps it is meant to be daring, but it just feels crass, like the filmmakers do not really care about their female characters or understand the gravity of what they are showing.

The troubling tone continues as the brothers kidnap ex-preacher Jacob (Harvey Keitel) and his children, Scott (Ernest Liu) and Kate (Juliette Lewis), forcing them to drive the pair to Mexico, with Richie clearly leering at Kate.
One ill-judged moment shows her propositioning him – in his mind. Another fetishises shots of her feet. But are these Richie’s preoccupations or Tarantino’s?
Once across the border, the group stops at the Titty Twister bar, where all hell breaks loose. After a risqué dance by Santanico Pandemonium (Salma Hayek), the staff turn into vampires, attacking and killing the patrons. Led by Seth and Jacob, a group of survivors attempts to last the night.
This is where the film really goes off the rails – and not in the way its makers intended. The writing is lazy and self-referential, such as introducing a character called Sex Machine (special effects legend Tom Savini), whose codpiece revolver is a nod to Rodriguez’s earlier work.

With the bar full of B-movie actors (stoner comedian Cheech Marin, blaxploitation star Fred Williamson) and semi-naked women, it resembles a Tarantino fever dream.
Despite stitching two different genres together, it seems Tarantino and Rodriguez did not give much thought to either, leaving the whole enterprise feeling rushed and slapdash.
Although the practical effects are good, the digital ones have aged terribly. The creatures have no lore, no mystique; they just appear and attack. And, as nothing has been invested in bringing them to life, it is hard to be engaged in watching them die.
Upon release, the film made a respectable US$59 million on a budget of US$19 million, and has since become a cult favourite – especially among men of a certain age. Two sequels, a television series and a video game followed, but the film’s greatest legacy is Ryan Coogler’s 2025 hit Sinners.

A film about criminals and vampires – but for adults – Sinners has the rare distinction of actually having something to say. Before it came out, Coogler wrote an open letter to thank everyone who had inspired him, Tarantino included. Perhaps he could teach Tarantino a thing or two about grace.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Ex-commerce chief Frederick Ma urges support for Hong Kong special needs education
data.description:
Former commerce chief Frederick Ma Si-hang has urged the public to deepen their support for disabled students in the city at a charity golf event organised by the Hong Kong Special Schools Council to raise awareness for this group.
The tournament, held on Friday at the Fanling Golf Club, drew 21 teams of 84 people, including one consisting of players with disabilities. Participants competed to win prizes such as a BYD electric car and tickets to see Cantopop singer Jacky Cheung Hok-yau in concert.
“The event is more than just money. We hope to raise public awareness for special schools … children with special needs are in need of much more care and support,” said Ma, who also chairs the special schools council and the Hong Kong Trade Development Council.
“The council did not have a very concrete goal this year. The ultimate goal for us is to call on more and more people to pay attention to children with special needs. The more, the merrier.”
The Hong Kong Special Schools Council is a non-profit organisation dedicated to advancing special education and supporting students with special needs.
Ma did not disclose how much funds had been raised from the tournament, whose participants included leaders from several financial institutions such as HSBC, the city’s biggest lender, and FWD Group, where Ma serves as chairman.
The Post learned that part of the participation fee would serve as a fund to support 62 schools that provide education to children with special needs.
According to Ma, the money will support events organised by the schools.
The council raised around HK$800,000 (US$102,680) last year through members’ donations. It was used to support 30 physically challenged children and their carers to go on a trip to Beijing.
“It was not easy to organise tours for children with special needs, as they could not go alone. They need their carers, which means the group would become bigger and need more money,” Ma said.
Andreas Ma Ho-him, 13, was one of the special needs players who joined the tournament. Starting playing golf at the age of three, the teenager said he had a blast on Friday.
“I really like the tournament. Thanks to the council, physically challenged people could get to experience something that they might not have experienced before and expand their visions,” he said.

data.title: Could Singapore remove Pritam Singh as Leader of the Opposition?
data.description:
In an unprecedented move in Singapore, Workers’ Party (WP) chief Pritam Singh could be stripped of his title as Leader of Opposition and lose his privileges when Singapore’s parliamentarians debate his suitability for the role next week, legal experts say.
Singh was the first to be appointed to the official role in 2020 by then prime minister Lee Hsien Loong after the general election that year, when the WP won a record 10 out of 93 seats.
On Friday, Leader of the House Indranee Rajah tabled a motion for next Tuesday or later in the week, calling on parliament to consider that Singh’s “conviction and conduct render him unsuitable to continue as the Leader of the Opposition” and to express regret at his conduct, which was “dishonourable and unbecoming” of an MP.
This comes after Singh, MP for Aljunied constituency, was convicted on two charges and fined S$14,000 (US$10,879) in February last year for lying under oath to a parliamentary committee looking into a lie former WP MP Raeesah Khan told in parliament in 2021. His conviction was upheld on December 4 after he appealed against it.
“The Leader of the Opposition is not a legal or constitutional office. It was created by parliament and can technically be removed by parliament as well. If parliament passes a resolution to strip Singh of the title, it will be done,” Kevin Tan, a constitutional law expert and adjunct professor at the National University of Singapore, told This Week in Asia.
Benjamin Joshua Ong, assistant professor of law at Singapore Management University, said there was no established precedent on whether the title of Leader of the Opposition could be revoked. “But my instinctive view is that given the weight that the title now carries, any revocation should be done only through a motion which parliament votes to pass.”
The outcome next week will be precedent-setting
Eugene Tan, law professor and political observer
Law professor and political observer Eugene Tan similarly noted that the appointment was not governed by the constitution, any law or parliamentary standing orders, and much would depend on Singh’s next move should parliament conclude he was unsuitable to continue in the role.
Should Singh not resign of his own accord, then Prime Minister Lawrence Wong’s hand could be forced, and Wong can be expected to revoke Singh as Leader of the Opposition.
“The outcome next week will be precedent-setting,” Eugene Tan said.
Singapore’s parliament is currently dominated by the People’s Action Party, which occupies 87 of the 97 elected seats, the remaining spots belonging to WP MPs.
As Leader of the Opposition, Singh’s privileges include an annual allowance of S$385,000, double that of an elected MP, and a separate allowance to hire up to three additional legislative assistants. He is also provided with an office and a secretary.
In parliament, he is generally given the right of first response among MPs and gets more time for his speeches.
According to a ministerial statement on the duties and privileges of the Leader of the Opposition in 2020, Singh also receives confidential government briefings on select matters of national security and external relations, and will be briefed in the event of a national crisis or emergency.

Jaclyn Neo, associate professor of law at the National University of Singapore, noted that while there were no specific written rules, it could be argued that there was a “constitutional convention” or informal rules outlining political costs instead of legal ones in the event of a breach.
“The position was formalised in parliament through ministerial speeches in 2020, and by now, one could argue that there is a constitutional convention for it,” Neo said.
She noted that the Leader of the Opposition role was distinct from Singh’s MP position, and he could not lose his seat unless it was vacated by the provisions set out in the constitution, which include him resigning, no longer being a citizen or a member of his political party.
Ong pointed out that, while rare, there was a procedure for parliament to expel an MP under the constitution.
Parliament must first state that the MP has committed “any dishonourable conduct, abuse of privilege or contempt”, then it must resolve to refer the matter to a select committee before parliament considers the report of the committee and decides whether the MP is indeed guilty. If found guilty, the individual might be fined, suspended or expelled by parliament, Ong said.
A WP spokesman said on Friday the party would address the matter during the parliamentary sitting, when the motion would be debated, and it did not have further comments to make at the time.
On January 3, WP said its central executive committee directed a disciplinary panel to be formed to determine if Singh had contravened the party’s constitution. There was also a request by cadres for a special conference to be held to discuss the issue, set to be called after the disciplinary panel has completed its work.

The party said that the disciplinary process would be concluded within three months, and notice of the special conference would be issued within two weeks after the conclusion of the disciplinary process.
Political scientist Bilveer Singh suggested that the party’s disciplinary hearing would unlikely result in Singh’s expulsion from WP, saying: “It’s very unlikely that the party will decide to kill itself.”
He also pointed out that how the PAP decided to vote during the debate would set a precedent, not only for the opposition but for its members too.
“Whatever PAP does, it sets the precedent for itself too. Whatever they do to him will also apply to themselves in the future if one of their members faces a similar situation,” Singh said.
Under Singapore’s constitution, an MP faces disqualification from parliament if convicted of an offence carrying a jail term of at least one year or a fine of S$10,000 or more.
Each count of lying under oath before a parliamentary committee carries a maximum penalty of a S$7,000 fine and three years’ imprisonment.
Singh received a S$7,000 fine for each of his two charges, and he was able to contest in the general election on May 3 last year, when he retained his parliamentary seat.

data.title: China’s yuan to strengthen to 6.85 per US dollar in first quarter of 2026: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Morgan Stanley has revised its outlook for the yuan, now forecasting it to strengthen to about 6.85 per US dollar in the first quarter of 2026 from a previous call of 7.05, before weakening slightly to about 7.0 by the end of the year.
The bank said it expects China’s “robust” exports through 2026 to 2027 to provide “a steady tailwind to nominal yuan”, with volumes projected to grow 5 to 6 per cent as the recovery in global demand broadens beyond tech and the country’s price competitiveness improves.
Morgan Stanley also attributed the revision to a modestly stronger yuan basket and near-term US dollar softness driven by Federal Reserve rate cuts, while cautioning that domestic deflation would constrain the yuan’s sustained appreciation.
On Friday, China’s central bank set the yuan’s daily midpoint fixing 69 pips stronger, at 7.0128 per dollar – its firmest level since September 30, 2024, marking a more than 15-month high.
The yuan has remained firm against the US dollar over the past month. Morgan Stanley attributed this mainly to “a seasonal increase in corporates’ foreign exchange conversion coupled with a strong trade surplus”, as Chinese exporters typically convert more foreign currency in December ahead of the financial year-end and the Lunar New Year.
Despite the upwards revision, the bank’s year-end projection of 7.0 is slightly weaker than the consensus of about 6.9. It pointed to “an appreciating dollar in 2H26 [the second half of 2026] and lingering domestic deflation challenges” amid a sluggish Chinese economy.
Dutch financial services firm ING said in a report on Monday that its dollar-yuan “fluctuation band” forecast for 2026 stood at 6.85 to 7.25, which it had adjusted “a little lower” amid recent appreciation.
In a note on Friday, Goldman Sachs Research said the yuan should expect a “gradual and choppy” appreciation going forward. Chinese export growth, “significant” foreign-exchange rate undervaluation and a “softer” US dollar would elevate the currency, it added.
The bank noted that the currency went below 7.0 yuan to the dollar last year, despite checks by the People’s Bank of China, citing dollar weakness and settlement demand.
A stronger yuan could help the internationalisation of the Chinese currency, though a sharp gain “would likely weigh on exporters’ profitability”, Goldman Sachs added.
“That said, when the dollar weakens, investors often seize the chance to position for a sharper (yuan) rally, leading to short-term overshoots and renewed policy management of the pace of appreciation.”
“Taken together, this should result in a choppy trajectory ahead.”

data.title: China’s J-35 jet maker flexes production muscle, pledging to double output in 5 years
data.description:
A Chinese defence giant has flexed its production muscle early in the new year by releasing a video of its star product, the J-35 stealth fighter, and pledging to double its warplane production over the next three to five years.
On Tuesday, a green-coated J-35 fighter roared down the runway at the Shenyang Aircraft Corporation (SAC) airfield in China’s northern Liaoning province, making its first flight of the year, according to a video released by the manufacturer, a subsidiary of Aviation Industry Corporation of China (AVIC), on the same day.
In separate images, SAC displayed two green-primed J-35 fighters parked at the airfield. They appeared to be fresh off the production line, lacking the tactical grey coating found on in-service aircraft.


The display appeared to showcase the steady production pace of the fifth-generation fighter following its official commissioning by the Chinese military last year.
According to a Liaoning Daily report on December 26, by the end of June last year, the main assembly plant at SAC’s new factory complex was completed, and product assembly officially had begun.
It is expected that the new factory will enter mass production this year. The report said that over the next three to five years, the factory would evolve into a production base with an “intelligent manufacturing and full chain” support system, doubling its total production capacity.
The SAC factory appears to be the same as one previously reported. In August 2023, a company notice said SAC planned to invest 8.6 billion yuan (US$1.2 billion) to build a new production site covering 4.2 sq km (1.6 square miles).
The report on December 26 said the new SAC factory was part of an entire “Shenyang Aerospace City” planned to cover 79.2 sq km, roughly the area of Hong Kong Island.
The new SAC factory complex is expected to include key facilities such as the final assembly plant and a runway that supports flight testing, and is under construction, according to the report.
SAC produces multiple models of fighter jets, including one of China’s most advanced carrier-based stealth fighters, the J-35. It is part of the air wing aboard China’s third aircraft carrier, the Fujian, which is also the first electromagnetic catapult-equipped carrier.




The J-35 completed initial catapult-assisted take-off and arrested landing training on the Fujian in September before the carrier officially entered service two months later.
The naval variant of the J-35, along with the J-35A air force variant, was showcased in China’s Victory Day military parade in Beijing in September, alongside a range of new aircraft, drones and missiles.
The recent flight of the green-coated aircraft strongly suggested that the J-35A had entered serial production and advanced flight testing, according to a report by Army Recognition online magazine.
The display marks another effort by Chinese manufacturers to showcase warplanes during the production phase, a move that was once rare but has become more common in the past year, reflecting Beijing’s ambitions in the ongoing defence capacity competition with the United States.


In July, at least two J-35 fighters, also showing the green primer, were spotted in a CCTV programme in a hangar at SAC, which is also the developer of the carrier-based J-15 and its new variant, the J-15T.
On Wednesday, CCTV said the green primer helped to protect the aircraft from rust and corrosion.
During the initial flight and early test phases, maintaining the aircraft in a simple green primer state allowed easier inspection and maintenance by engineers, the state broadcaster said. It also helped cut costs because painting the final tactical coat before the design was finalised would be a waste if changes were needed later, it said.

data.title: Why Ashley Tisdale called her mommy group ‘too high school’, and how Hilary Duff’s hubby responded
data.description:
There are some things that make a person happy they’re not a millennial mum. Ashley Tisdale’s mommy group drama is one of those things. Because Tisdale – now Ashley Tisdale French – sounds like she might be stuck in her own High School Musical, and it looks as if Hilary Duff’s husband just threw a stink bomb under the bleachers.
Adding to the drama: Duff and Mandy Moore are rumoured to be part of the group, though Tisdale French, 40, has refused to name names.

“Since becoming a public figure as a teenager, it’s often the thing I least expect that people most want to talk about,” the former child star wrote in an essay for New York Magazine that echoes what she wrote a while back on her own blog. “Sometimes, I’ll say something offhandedly, only to see it turn into a headline or start a conversation on TikTok.”
Bottom line, per the essay, is that Tisdale French – who married composer Christopher French in 2014 – was pregnant during the pandemic. She missed out on baby showers and prenatal yoga classes and handing her newborn baby off to acquaintances. Then a friend brought together a group of new mums.
“[F]inally, we were able to be together, and our kids were able to be together, and it all felt right,” she wrote. The founder of the Being Frenshe line of personal care products thought she had joined a group of cool kids who did cool things.

“I felt energised by being around women who understood the challenge of feeding a baby while taking a Zoom call.
“[I]t made me hopeful about finding the balance between fulfilling work and family life, since all these cool women were able to do it. Maybe we’d be able to share our secrets to success.”
Then social media burst her bubble.
“I remember being left out of a couple of group hangs, and I knew about them because Instagram made sure it fed me every single photo and Instagram Story.”
She wrote that she realised her mommy group was just like high school.

“Even though it had been decades since 10th grade, the experience of being left out felt so similar.”
But now she was a grown-up, so she took a stand.
“So that’s exactly what I texted to the group after being left out from yet another group hang: ‘This is too high school for me and I don’t want to take part in it anymore.’”
People didn’t react well, she said. One mommy sent flowers, then didn’t acknowledge her thank you. Another was like, “You weren’t invited? I thought you were.”
Keep in mind, her essay is part of a series titled It’s Been a Year, which includes pieces about a woman learning via DNA test results that she wasn’t the person she thought she was and one from actor Rebecca Gayheart about going through estranged husband Eric Dane’s ALS diagnosis and subsequent care.
But back to Tisdale French.

She was more specific about what happened in her older blog post, by the way.
“I realised that there were group text chains that didn’t include everyone, which led to cliques forming within the larger group. And after the third or fourth time of seeing social media photos of everyone else at a hangout that I didn’t get invited to, it felt like I wasn’t really part of the group after all.”
She also shared a revelation with her blog readers.
“If a mom group consistently leaves you feeling hurt, drained or left out, it’s not the mom group for you. (Even if it used to be!) It’s no longer serving you in a way that lifts you up, and you don’t have to stay out of obligation or anything else.”

We will never know how far into either essay Duff’s husband got. We do know that Matthew Koma didn’t hesitate to pull out the Burn Book.
Koma apparently got riled up enough over it that on Instagram, he mimicked Tisdale French’s repost of New York Magazine’s promotional post about the essay, slapping a picture of his own face over hers and changing the headline from “Breaking Up With My Toxic Mom Group” to his own: “When You’re The Most Self Obsessed Tone Deaf Person On Earth, Other Moms Tend To Shift Focus To Their Actual Toddlers”, with “A Mom Group Tell All Through A Father’s Eyes” as a subheadline.
Alas, he posted it as an Instagram Story, now expired, so we can imagine it only with the help of outlets such as People, which for the longest while has been writing about Reddit AITA posts and the subsequent comments telling the original poster whether they are indeed the jerk in a particular situation. (Not that any jerks are being discussed here.)

Tisdale French doesn’t name names in her essay, but Koma’s reaction seems to indicate that former child star Duff, 38, might have been one of the allegedly mean mums who was definitely not being named. And Duff and Koma hosted former child star Moore, 41, and her family after last year’s Eaton fire in Altadena, California, when Moore’s home burned down, so some might bet on Moore also being among the mothers of small children in former child star Tisdale French’s group.
Tisdale French, meanwhile, apparently anticipated this kind of speculation in reaction to the New York essay because she had experienced it after blogging about the same topic. And apparently it’s all wrong, wrong, wrong.
“It’s a subject that has made women DM me to say ‘I feel seen’ and to share their most emotional stories with me,” she wrote for the magazine.

“It has also made wannabe online sleuths try to do some investigating like they’re on CSI (please, don’t even try – whatever you think is true isn’t even close).”
Cool? Uncool? Christopher French, Ashley’s husband, may have made his own decision on that already.
“Underrated life skill,” French wrote in an Instagram Story, quoting author and mindfulness coach Cory Allen. “Pausing to decide if it’s worth your energy.”

data.title: US pledges US$45 million to bolster Thailand, Cambodia truce amid Trump peace claims
data.description:
The United States on Friday announced some US$45 million in aid as it tries to bolster a fragile truce between Thailand and Cambodia.
Michael DeSombre, the top State Department official for East Asia, was visiting Thailand and Cambodia to discuss ways to strengthen the ceasefire, which US President Donald Trump has sought to highlight as an achievement.
DeSombre said the United States would offer US$20 million to help both countries combat drug trafficking and cyber scams, which have become a major concern in Cambodia.
He also said the United States would give US$15 million to help support people displaced by the recent fighting as well as US$10 million for demining.
“The United States will continue to support the Cambodian and Thai governments as they implement the Kuala Lumpur Peace Accords and pave the way for a return to peace, prosperity and stability for their people and the region,” DeSombre said in a statement.


He was referring to an agreement signed between the two countries in the presence of Trump during an October visit to Malaysia, then head of the Asean regional bloc.
Major new clashes erupted last month. The two sides reached a truce on December 27, after three weeks of fighting, although Thailand accused Cambodia of violating an apparent accidental fire.
Cambodia has called on Thailand to pull out its forces from several border areas that Phnom Penh claims as its own

The long-standing conflict stems from a dispute over the colonial-era demarcation of their 800km (500-mile) border, where both sides claim territory and centuries-old temple ruins.
Trump has listed the conflict as one of a number of wars he says he has solved as he loudly insists he deserves the Nobel Peace Prize.
Trump on taking office drastically slashed foreign aid, including for months freezing long-standing assistance to Cambodia for demining, with the administration saying it will provide money only in support of narrow US interests.

data.title: Philippine teen arrested over alleged US$845 baby sale as police warn of rise in cases
data.description:
A teenager in the Philippines has been arrested for trying to sell her one-month-old baby for 50,000 Philippine pesos (US$845) on social media, as police warn that similar cases have been “spreading like wildfire” in recent years.
The 17-year-old was caught on Tuesday after police officers contacted her in response to her online post and arranged a meeting, according to the Philippine Daily Inquirer.
“We found out from her mother that the teenager had spent the money for her tuition. So her plan was to use the money she would get from selling her baby to cover her tuition,” Sheila Portento, chief of the police’s Women and Children’s Protection Centre, told the newspaper.
The mother and baby were later handed over to the Quezon City Social Services Department.


Investigators also found that the teenager had been communicating with a person in Japan who had promised to send her money in exchange for the baby, police said.
While the teenager is considered a minor under the Juvenile Justice Act – which says children aged 15 to 18 cannot be held criminally liable unless they acted ‘with discernment’ – she may still face prosecution for human trafficking, child abuse and cybercrime, according to Portento.
“Her discernment, based on the certification of the social worker who conducted the assessment, was above average. So, definitely, she was aware [of what she was doing]. She planned and executed it,” Portento said.
The baby’s father, who is also a minor, is being investigated for his role in the case.
Portento cited the arrest as an example of a broader trend, saying the number of online baby-selling cases recorded by police had risen from five in 2024 to eight in 2025.
“It seems to have suddenly spread like wildfire because online platforms are very accessible. You can sell almost anything and it’s so alarming,” she said.
“There are many sites online that we cyber patrol … We are closely coordinating with social media platforms and requesting them to take down the posts selling children.”
Financial hardship was the most commonly cited reason for selling babies, Portento said.
She added that parents who could not afford to care for their children should instead turn to legal adoption channels through the National Authority for Child Care.
“They can just surrender the child without profiting off them,” she said.
In June, the authority found 12 social media groups selling babies online, each with more than 200,000 followers, according to the Philippine Star.
The Commission on Human Rights raised the alarm on the rising number of online baby-selling cases a month later, after a one-month-old infant who was offered for sale for 90,000 pesos was rescued in Pasay City.
Most babies sold online were trafficked by their own families, the commission said.
“The parents are complicit because they agree to sell their babies, and unfortunately, the reason they often give is poverty, which pushes them to do it or to sell their own children,” it told the newspaper.

The commission called on the government to “strengthen [the] monitoring and regulation of online platforms, enhance surveillance and investigative capabilities and ensure the swift prosecution and conviction of perpetrators”, according to the Inquirer report.
“No child should ever be bought, sold, or treated as a commodity,” it said in a statement.
Earlier this week, the government launched the Psychosocial Support and Other Interventions for Adolescent Mothers and their Families Project (ProtecTeen) to help teenage mothers.
“While the National Authority for Child Care is firm in its campaign against illegal adoption and child trafficking and exploitation through online baby selling, we also need to underscore the critical importance of providing support mechanisms for adolescent mothers,” Undersecretary Janella Estrada said in a statement.
“The goal of ProtecTeen is to empower young mothers, engage their families and create a supportive environment to ensure that they can navigate their circumstances and become productive members of society, thereby safeguarding their rights and the future of their children.”

data.title: Ex-Taiwan model, actress Jean Wang ends abusive marriage, excels in culinary arts
data.description:
Taiwan actress and ex-model Jean Wang escaped an unhappy and abusive marriage to rebuild a career using her master culinary skills.
Wang, 55, debuted as a model at the age of 15. She was once hailed as Taiwan’s most beautiful woman and was soon snapped up by the film industry.

She played a main character in the 1993 Hong Kong film The East is Red together with legendary actresses Brigitte Lin and Joey Wong. She also played an important supporting character in the wuxia film series Once Upon a Time.
In 1996, she was offered the part of the main character in Chen Kaige’s Temptress Moon, but was replaced by Gong Li reportedly because of the director’s strict demands for acting skills.
Wang then slowly faded from show business. In 2004, she married a fu’erdai, or the son of a rich family, Chen Wei-Tao, and had a son with him.

Chen was violent and abusive to her, even physically assaulting her a day before she gave birth.
Chen’s family blamed it on Wang’s postnatal depression and “her lack of respect for her mother-in-law”.
However, Wang forgave him after he sent her 101 roses.

He continued abusing her, reportedly installing an audio monitor in her car. He also cheated on her.
In 2008, Wang finally decided to leave her husband and 14 months later she successfully divorced him and got joint custody of her son.
Wang then became a role model for women in abusive relationships.
She also changed her values, thinking of divorce not as shameful but as a “new start”.
“Do not depend on anyone else,” she once said.

In her 50s, Wang said that after all she had been through, she had more time to spend with herself and ended up knowing herself better.
Wang also studied culinary arts in Japan and shone on stage with her patisserie work.
Wang continued her acting career, too. She starred in the hit Netflix Taiwan TV series Light the Night in 2021 and 2022.
According to the Supreme People’s Procuratorate, 30 per cent of married women in China are victims of domestic violence at the hands of their husbands.
“She was right to leave him. Abusive men will never change,” said one online observer.
